title: Victoria sponge
subtitle: Classic equal-weight British layer cake with jam and buttercream
excerpt: Equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and self-raising flour beaten together, baked in two 20 cm sandwich tins, then sandwiched with raspberry jam and vanilla buttercream. The simplest of the British layer cakes and the one that everything else is measured against.
sourceNotes: The Victoria sponge is named after Queen Victoria and appears in domestic cookery literature from the 1860s onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records a Victoria sandwich cake made by the all-in-one equal-weight method. The defining features are the equal-weight formula (eggs weighed in their shells to set all other quantities), self-raising flour, and the jam sandwich. The buttercream filling is a later addition; the original recipe used jam alone.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled sponge layers individually for up to 2 months. Defrost and fill on the day of serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponge the day before; wrap in cling film and fill on the day. Unfilled sponge keeps better than the assembled cake.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. A fan oven cooks the sponge more evenly across both tins on the same shelf.

recipeTools.notes: two 20 cm sandwich tins, greased and base-lined

glossaryTerms.definition: A baking formula in which the butter, sugar, eggs, and flour are all weighed to match one another. The traditional way is to weigh the eggs in their shells first, then use that weight as the measure for the other three ingredients. This produces a rich, tender sponge that adapts to any egg size.

content.text: The Victoria sponge is the foundational British cake. Its formula, the

, is scalable to any egg size: weigh four eggs in their shells, then use that weight for butter, caster sugar, and self-raising flour. The batter should be a soft dropping consistency; if it falls heavily from the spoon, add a tablespoon of warm milk. The sponge should spring back cleanly when pressed in the centre.

attrs.prepNote: if needed to loosen the batter

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together until pale and fluffy, at least 4 minutes by hand or 2 minutes with a mixer. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each. If the mixture curdles, add a spoonful of flour. Add the vanilla extract.

Sift over the flour and baking powder. Fold in with a large spatula in as few strokes as possible until just combined. The batter should drop from the spatula in a slow ribbon. If it is too stiff, stir in up to 2 tablespoons of milk. Divide evenly between the two tins and level the surface with the back of the spoon. Bake for 23 to 26 minutes until risen, golden, and springy to a light touch in the centre. A skewer should come out clean. Leave in the tins for 5 minutes, then cool completely on a rack.

For the buttercream, beat the softened butter until very pale and fluffy. Add the sifted icing sugar in two additions, beating well between each. Add the vanilla. To assemble: place the flatter sponge layer on a plate, spread with jam, then spoon and spread the buttercream over it. Top with the second sponge, dome-side up. Dust with icing sugar to finish.
